risks: Stripe webhook reliability: payment status updates depend on webhook delivery; requires idempotency keys, retry handling, and reconciliation logic to avoid missed or duplicate Paid events
PDF generation at scale: server-side PDF rendering (e.g., Puppeteer or WeasyPrint) is memory-intensive; needs queuing or a dedicated rendering service to avoid blocking the main API under load
Client portal authentication-free access: public invoice links need expiry logic, rate limiting, and care around exposing financial data — token design must balance convenience with security
Recurring invoice scheduling: cron-based scheduling must handle timezone edge cases, failed sends, and idempotency so invoices are never duplicated on retry after a job failure
features: Create and send branded PDF invoices via email
Client address book with contact and billing details
Line item builder with quantity, rate, and tax support
Invoice status tracking (Draft, Sent, Viewed, Paid, Overdue)
Automatic overdue detection and reminder emails
Payment integration (Stripe) for online payment links on invoices
Dashboard with revenue summary, outstanding balance, and overdue count
Multi-currency support with exchange rate display
Client portal for viewing and paying invoices without login
Tax report export (annual summary by client)
Custom invoice templates and branding (logo, colors)
userFlows: User signs up → selects plan → completes profile (name, business name, payment details) → lands on dashboard

User creates client → creates invoice → adds line items → applies tax rate → previews PDF → sends via email → client receives link → client views invoice (status: Viewed) → client pays via Stripe → invoice marked Paid → user notified
User creates invoice → sends → invoice passes due date unpaid → system marks Overdue → reminder email sent to client → client disputes → user manually updates notes
User enables recurring invoice for a client → sets frequency and start date → system auto-generates invoice on schedule → sends automatically → user reviews in dashboard
User tries to delete a client with unpaid invoices → warning shown listing outstanding invoices → must mark invoices paid/void or reassign before deletion proceeds
Client receives invoice email → clicks link → views invoice in client portal (no login required) → pays via embedded Stripe checkout → payment confirmed → invoice status updates in real time
constraints: PDF generation must complete in under 3 seconds
Invoice unique share links must be publicly accessible without authentication
API responses under 300ms p95 for dashboard and invoice list endpoints
Email delivery via transactional provider (e.g., Resend or SendGrid) with delivery logging
Stripe webhook processing must be idempotent to prevent duplicate payment records
Core invoice creation flow completable in under 5 user interactions
Must support at least USD, EUR, GBP, CAD currencies at launch
description: A SaaS invoicing platform built for independent freelancers to create, send, and track invoices and payments. It reduces the administrative burden of freelance work by centralizing client management, invoice lifecycle tracking, and payment status in one tool — eliminating spreadsheet-based workarounds.
mvpFeatures: Create invoices with line items (description, quantity, rate, tax) and generate PDF
Send invoice via email with a unique shareable link
Manual payment marking + Stripe payment link integration
Invoice status lifecycle (Draft → Sent → Viewed → Paid/Overdue)
openQuestions: Is this single-tenant per freelancer, or will team/agency accounts with multiple users sharing clients and invoices be required at launch? This changes the data model's ownership and permission layer significantly.
Should partial payments be supported (e.g., a client pays 50% now, 50% later)? This affects the Payment entity design, invoice status logic, and how 'Paid' is defined.

Will the app need to comply with specific regional invoicing regulations (e.g., EU VAT invoice requirements, GST in Australia)? This impacts required invoice fields, tax logic complexity, and potentially report formats.
